perturbation.original_text: Racing shall obtain and maintain, at Racing's expense, comprehensive automobile liability insurance covering all owned, non-owned and hired vehicles used by Racing in the Business with limits of not less than $5,000,000 per occurrence combined single limit for personal injury and property damage, including all statutory coverage for all states of operation. Racing shall also provide comprehensive (fire and theft) and collision insurance on each vehicle used in the Business.
perturbation.changed_text: Racing shall obtain and maintain, at Racing's expense, basic automobile liability insurance covering all owned, non-owned and hired vehicles used by Racing in the Business with limits of not less than $100,000 per occurrence combined single limit for personal injury and property damage, and no statutory coverage for any state of operation. Racing shall also provide fire insurance on each vehicle used in the Business.
perturbation.explanation: The terms 'comprehensive automobile liability insurance' and 'collision insurance' have been replaced with 'basic automobile liability insurance' and 'fire insurance', respectively. The liability limit has been drastically reduced, and the requirement of 'statutory coverage for all states of operation' has been removed. This creates a conflict, as many states have minimum liability coverage requirements that are higher than $100,000 and mandate certain coverages.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Texas minimum liability insurance requirements and other states' minimum coverage requirements
perturbation.law_citation: Texas Transportation Code § 601.072 and similar statutes in other states
perturbation.law_explanation: Texas Transportation Code § 601.072 mandates minimum liability coverage amounts. The changed text specifies a lower amount and eliminates statutory coverage for all states of operation, directly contradicting Texas law and potentially the laws of other states where the racing team operates. Also the 'fire insurance' is not enough for vehicles.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: artnership, association, or corporation. (11) "State" means: (A) a state, territory, or possession of the United States; or (B) the District of Columbia. Text of subdivision effective until July 01, 2025 (12) "Vehicle registration" means: (A) a registration certificate, registration receipt, or number plate issued under Chapter 502 ; or (B) a dealer's license plate or temporary tag issued under Chapter 503 . Text of subdivision effective on July 01, 2025 (12) "Vehicle registration" means: (A) a registration certificate, registration receipt, or number plate issued under Chapter 502 ; or (B) a dealer's license plate issued under Chapter 503 . Acts 1995, 74th Leg., ch. 165, Sec. 1, eff. Sept. 1, 1995. Amended by Acts 2003, 78th Leg., ch. 1318, Sec. 6, eff. Sept. 1, 2003. Amended by: Acts 2009, 81st Leg., R.S., Ch. 793 (S.B. 1235 ), Sec. 15, eff. September 1, 2009. Acts 2023, 88th Leg., R.S., Ch. 668 (H.B. 718 ), Sec. 31, eff. July 1, 2025. Acts 2023, 88th Leg., R.S., Ch. 709 (H.B. 2190 ), Sec. 62, eff. September 1, 2023. Sec. 601.003. JUDGMENT; SATISFIED JUDGMENT. (a) For purposes of this chapter, judgment refers only to a final judgment that is no longer appealable or has been finally affirmed on appeal and that was rendered by a court of any state, a province of Canada, or the United States on a cause of action: (1) for damages for bodily injury, death, or damage to or destruction of property arising out of the ownership, maintenance, or use of a motor vehicle; or (2) on an agreement of settlement for damages for bodily injury, death, or damage to or destruction of property arising out of the ownership, maintenance, or use of a motor vehicle. (b) For purposes of this chapter, a judgment is considered to be satisfied as to the appropriate part of the judgment set out by this subsection if: (1) the total amount credited on one or more judgments for bodily injury to or death of one person resulting from one collision equals or exceeds the amount required under Section 601.072 (a-1)(1) to establish financial responsibility; (2) the total amount credited on one or more judgments for bodily injury to or death of two or more persons resulting from one collision equals or exceeds the amount required under Section 601.072 (a-1)(2) to establish financial responsibility; or (3) the total amount credited on one or more judgments for damage to or destruction of property of another resulting from one collision equals or exceeds the amount required under Section 601.072 (a-1)(3) to establish financial responsibility. (c) In determining whether a judgment is satisfied under Subsection (b), a payment made in settlement of a claim for damages for bodily injury, death, or damage to or destruction of property is considered to be an amount credited on a judgment. (d) For purposes of this section: (1) damages for bodily injury or death include damages for care and loss of services; and (2) damages for damage to or destruction of property include damages for loss of use. Acts 1995, 74th Leg., ch. 165, Sec. 1, eff. Sept. 1, 1995. Amended by: Acts 2023, 88th Leg., R.S., Ch. 709 (H.B. 2190 ), Sec. 63, eff. September 1, 2023. Sec. 601.005. EVIDENCE IN CIVIL SUIT. A person at a trial for damages may not refer to or offer as evidence of the negligence or due care of a party: (1) an action taken by the department under this chapter; (2) the findings on which that action is based; or (3) the security or evidence of financial responsibility filed under this chapter. Acts 1995, 74th Leg., ch. 165, Sec. 1, eff. Sept. 1, 1995. Amended by: Acts 2017, 85th Leg., R.S., Ch. 533 (S.B. 312 ), Sec. 50, eff. September 1, 2017. Sec. 601.006. APPLICABILITY TO CERTAIN OWNERS AND OPERATORS. If an owner or operator of a motor vehicle involved in a collision in this state does not have a driver's license or vehicle registration or is a nonresident, the person may not be issued a driver's license or registration until the person has complied with this chapter to the same extent that
perturbation.justification: The changed text creates a contradiction because it reduces the liability coverage to $100,000 and eliminates statutory coverage for all states, while the original text mandated comprehensive automobile liability insurance with a $5,000,000 limit and statutory coverage for all states. The Law Explanation states this contradicts Texas Transportation Code § 601.072, which mandates minimum liability coverage amounts. The first snippet, Texas Transportation Code § 601.003, defines judgments related to motor vehicle accidents and refers to Section 601.072 for financial responsibility requirements. Although snippet 2 is missing, the reduction in coverage and elimination of statutory requirements directly undermines the financial responsibility mandates implied by 601.003 and referred to by the Law Explanation, indicating a strong contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Racing agrees to indemnify the Sponsor and its officers, directors, agents and employees and to hold them harmless from any loss, claim, cost, damage or liability in excess of the Maximum Obligation which (i) the Sponsor shall incur as a result of this Agreement, or (ii) arises from any failure by Racing to perform any of its obligations hereunder.
perturbation.changed_text: Racing agrees to possibly indemnify the Sponsor and its officers, directors, agents and employees and to hold them harmless from any loss, claim, cost, damage or liability in excess of the Maximum Obligation which (i) the Sponsor shall incur as a result of this Agreement, or (ii) arises from any failure by Racing to maybe perform any of its obligations hereunder.
perturbation.explanation: The terms 'agrees to indemnify' and 'to perform any of its obligations' were changed to 'agrees to possibly indemnify' and 'maybe perform any of its obligations', which weakens the contractual obligation of indemnification and performance of agreed duties, creating uncertainty and making it difficult to enforce.  Indemnification agreements must be clear and unambiguous.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Contract Law principles regarding certainty and definiteness of terms
perturbation.law_citation: Erie R. Co. v. Tompkins, 304 U.S. 64 (1938)
perturbation.law_explanation: The alteration introduces uncertainty in the contractual obligations, potentially rendering the indemnification clause unenforceable. Basic contract law requires that the terms of a contract be reasonably certain. The modified language now makes the duty to indemnify discretionary, contradicting basic principles of contract enforceability established in cases like Erie R. Co. v. Tompkins.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: ERIE R. CO. v. TOMPKINS. | Supreme Court | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you ERIE R. CO. v. TOMPKINS. Supreme Court 304 U.S. 64 58 S.Ct. 817 82 L.Ed. 1188 ERIE R. CO. v. TOMPKINS. * No. 367. Argued Jan. 31, 1938. Decided April 25, 1938. Messrs. Theodore Kiendl, Harold W. Bissell, and William C. Cannon, all of New York City, for petitioner. [Argument of Counsel from pages 65-67 intentionally omitted] Messrs. Fred H. Rees, Alexander L. Strouse, and Bernard G. Nemeroff, all of New York City (Bernard Kaufman and William Walsh, both of New York City, and Aaron L. Danzig, of Jamaica, L.I., on the brief) for respondent. [Argument of Counsel from Page 68 intentionally omitted] Mr. Justice BRANDEIS delivered the opinion of the Court. 1 The question for decision is whether the oft-challenged doctrine of Swift v. Tyson 1 shall now be disapproved. 2 Tompkins, a citizen of Pennsylvania, was injured on a dark night by a passing freight train of the Erie Railroad Company while walking along its right of way at Hughestown in that state. He claimed that the accident occurred through negligence in the operation, or maintenance, of the train; that he was rightfully on the premises as licensee because on a commonly used beaten footpath which ran for a short distance alongside the tracks; and that he was struck by something which looked like a door projecting from one of the moving cars. To enforce that claim he brought an action in the federal court for Southern New York, which had jurisdiction because the company is a corporation of that state. It denied liability; and the case was tried by a jury. 3 The Erie insisted that its duty to Tompkins was no greater than that owed to a trespasser. It contended, among other things, that its duty to Tompkins, and hence its liability, should be determined in accordance with the Pennsylvania law; that under the law of Pennsylvania, as declared by its highest court, persons who use pathways along the railroad right of way—that is, a longitudinal pathway as distinguished from a crossing—are to be deemed trespassers; and that the railroad is not liable for injuries to undiscovered trespassers resulting from its negligence, unless it be wanton or willful. T
perturbation.justification: The original text clearly states Racing's agreement to indemnify and perform obligations. The changed text introduces 'possibly indemnify' and 'maybe perform,' which creates uncertainty. The Law Explanation argues this uncertainty renders the indemnification clause unenforceable, conflicting with contract law's requirement for reasonably certain terms. While the scraped snippets do not directly address indemnity, the Erie R. Co. v. Tompkins snippet mentions the need to determine liability according to applicable law, suggesting a standard of legal enforceability. The weakening of obligations from definite to possible introduces ambiguity that could render the obligations unenforceable. The change directly undermines the original intent and enforceability of the indemnification clause, creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Conduct. Racing and all Racing members, including but not limited to all drivers, agree to use best efforts to conduct themselves in such a manner so as not to reflect unfavorably upon the Sponsor or its products.
perturbation.changed_text: Conduct. Racing and all Racing members, including but not limited to all drivers, are encouraged to conduct themselves in such a manner so as to try not to reflect unfavorably upon the Sponsor or its products.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'agree to use best efforts' to 'are encouraged to conduct themselves' weakens the obligation. 'Best efforts' is a legally recognized standard, while 'encouraged' is vague and unenforceable, and 'try not to' is not definitive.  This introduces ambiguity and reduces the sponsor's protection.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Contract Law principles regarding the duty of good faith and fair dealing
perturbation.law_explanation: By changing 'agree to use best efforts' to 'are encouraged to conduct themselves' and 'try not to,' the contract now contradicts the implied duty of good faith and fair dealing present in all contracts. The modified text suggests a weaker, non-binding obligation, which undermines the sponsor's expectation that Racing will genuinely try to avoid negative publicity. This contradicts Restatement (Second) of Contracts § 205.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: good faith | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you good faith Good faith is a broad term that’s used to encompass honest dealing. Depending on the exact setting, good faith may require an honest belief or purpose, faithful performance of duties , observance of fair dealing standards, or an absence of fraudulent intent . A fiduciary relationship creates a duty of good faith between the agent and the principal . The breach of this duty of good faith can lead to liability . Failure to act in good faith is known as bad faith and is generally considered to be a level of culpability greater than negligence . [Last reviewed in January of 2023 by the Wex Definitions Team ] Wex COMMERCE business law contracts wex definitions business sectors legal education and practice Wex Toolbox

perturbation.justification: The original text uses 'agree to use best efforts,' a legally recognized standard implying a firm commitment. The changed text weakens this obligation to 'are encouraged to conduct themselves' and 'try not to,' introducing ambiguity and reducing enforceability. This contradicts the implied duty of good faith and fair dealing, undermining the sponsor's expectation of avoiding negative publicity, per the law explanation. Scraped Law Snippet 1 defines 'good faith' as encompassing 'honest dealing' and 'faithful performance of duties.' The modified text's weaker language suggests a lack of genuine effort, conflicting with the requirement of good faith.
